enrichment.short_description: Scaleway is a French cloud infrastructure provider offering compute, storage, AI, and managed services with transparent, pay-as-you-go pricing.
enrichment.pricing_model: Pay-as-you-go / usage-based, plus dedicated server plans
enrichment.starting_price: From about EUR 4.99/month for entry-level Dedibox dedicated servers; cloud instances billed per-second from fractions of a euro per hour
enrichment.seo_title: Scaleway Review 2026 - Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what Scaleway costs in 2026, who it is for, and how its cloud compute, storage, and AI infrastructure compares to other European cloud providers.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Scaleway is a European cloud computing and infrastructure provider headquartered in Paris, France, and operated as a majority-owned subsidiary of the Iliad Group, the telecom conglomerate founded by Xavier Niel. The company traces its roots to 1999, when it launched as Online SAS (Online.net), a web hosting and domain registration business; in April 2015 its infrastructure-as-a-service offering was rebranded as Scaleway, and the brand has since expanded into a full public cloud platform. Scaleway runs its own data centers across Paris, Amsterdam, and Warsaw, and reports serving more than 25,000 businesses across roughly 160 countries, with a workforce that has grown past 500 employees. Its product catalog spans bare-metal Dedibox dedicated servers, virtual compute instances (categorized into Development, General Purpose, Compute Optimized, and Memory Optimized ranges), GPU instances for AI and machine learning workloads, managed Kubernetes, serverless functions and containers, object and block storage, managed databases, and networking services such as load balancers and private networks. Pricing is usage-based and marketed as transparent, with dedicated Dedibox servers starting around EUR 4.99 per month and cloud compute instances billed by the second at low hourly rates, positioning Scaleway as a cost-competitive alternative to AWS, Google Cloud, and Azure, particularly for European companies with data-sovereignty requirements under GDPR. As part of the Iliad Group, Scaleway has increasingly emphasized sovereign and AI-focused infrastructure, including GPU clusters for AI training and inference, and has pursued acquisitions (such as data platform company Saagie) to expand its AI and data engineering capabilities. The company has also explored spinning off parts of its data center operations into a standalone entity. For a directory like gappsy.com, Scaleway is best categorized alongside other cloud infrastructure and hosting providers (AWS, Google Cloud, DigitalOcean, Hetzner, OVHcloud) as a mid-size, France-based, developer-oriented cloud with a strong emphasis on European sovereignty, straightforward pricing, and growing AI infrastructure offerings. It is not a SaaS application but core infrastructure (IaaS/PaaS), so it is most relevant to developers, DevOps teams, and companies evaluating cloud hosting alternatives to the US hyperscalers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Scaleway is a cloud infrastructure provider based in Paris, France, and a subsidiary of the Iliad Group. Originally founded in 1999 as Online SAS, the company rebranded its cloud arm as Scaleway in 2015 and has since built a full public cloud platform spanning compute, storage, networking, AI, and managed services, serving tens of thousands of businesses worldwide.

Scaleway positions itself as a European alternative to the US hyperscalers, emphasizing data sovereignty, GDPR compliance, and transparent pricing. It operates its own data centers in France, the Netherlands, and Poland.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Scaleway's core offerings include bare-metal Dedibox dedicated servers, virtual compute instances across several performance tiers, managed Kubernetes and container services, serverless functions, object and block storage, managed databases, and networking tools like load balancers and private networks.

In recent years Scaleway has expanded aggressively into AI infrastructure, offering GPU instances for training and inference and pursuing acquisitions such as data platform company Saagie to strengthen its AI and data engineering stack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Scaleway uses a pay-as-you-go, usage-based pricing model with per-second billing on most cloud compute resources, meaning customers only pay for what they use without long-term commitments. Dedicated Dedibox servers start at roughly EUR 4.99 per month, while cloud instance pricing varies by CPU, memory, and GPU configuration.

The company markets its pricing as transparent and predictable, without the hidden egress fees and complex tiering often associated with larger cloud providers, though exact costs still depend on the specific product line and region selected.

features.description: Managed PostgreSQL, MySQL, and Redis-compatible database services with automated backups and scaling.

features.title: Networking tools
features.description: Load balancers, private networks, and VPC-style isolation for securing cloud architectures.

features.title: European data residency
features.description: Data centers located in Paris, Amsterdam, and Warsaw to support GDPR compliance and data sovereignty needs.

features.title: Per-second billing
features.description: Usage-based pricing billed by the second on most compute products, avoiding long-term lock-in.

European data centers support GDPR and data sovereignty requirements
Broad product range spanning bare metal to managed Kubernetes and AI GPUs
Backed by the well-capitalized Iliad Group, providing financial stability
Competitive pricing versus AWS, Google Cloud, and Azure for comparable resources
cons: Smaller global data center footprint than the major US hyperscalers
Fewer managed service integrations and third-party marketplace tools than AWS or Azure
Documentation and community support are smaller in scale than larger providers
Some advanced enterprise features require contacting sales rather than self-service

faqs.answer: Scaleway is headquartered in Paris, France, and is a majority-owned subsidiary of the Iliad Group.

faqs.answer: Scaleway uses pay-as-you-go, usage-based pricing billed per second for most cloud compute products, with dedicated Dedibox servers starting around EUR 4.99 per month.

faqs.question: Is Scaleway a good alternative to AWS or Google Cloud
faqs.answer: Scaleway is often chosen by European companies seeking GDPR-friendly data residency and simpler, more transparent pricing than the major US hyperscalers, though it has a smaller global footprint.

faqs.answer: Yes, Scaleway offers GPU instances designed for AI training and inference as part of its expanding AI infrastructure lineup.

faqs.answer: Scaleway is a majority-owned subsidiary of the Iliad Group, the French telecom conglomerate founded by Xavier Niel.

enrichment.short_description: Sched is event and conference scheduling software that helps organizers build personalized agendas, manage speakers, and handle registration for in-person, virtual, and hybrid events.
enrichment.pricing_model: Annual subscription tiers plus a free plan for small events
enrichment.seo_title: Sched Review 2026 - Event Scheduling Software Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: An overview of Sched's event and conference scheduling platform, including pricing tiers, key features, and what kinds of events it fits best.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Sched, found at sched.com, is event and conference scheduling software founded in 2008 and based in Los Angeles, California. It is built specifically for organizers of conferences, conventions, festivals, and other multi-session events who need to manage complex agendas, speaker lineups, sponsor profiles, and attendee registration in one platform. Core functionality includes a personalized schedule builder that lets attendees create their own agenda from many concurrent sessions, a web and mobile app for browsing the event, speaker and session profile pages, registration and ticketing tools, call-for-papers/session submission workflows, attendee networking features, badge printing, and lead retrieval tools for sponsors and exhibitors. Sched structures its pricing into named annual plans rather than simple monthly SaaS tiers: the Launch plan starts at roughly $600 per year, Boost (the most popular tier) runs about $1,500 per year and adds attendee networking, attendance tracking, customizable badges, sponsor profiles, and enhanced call-for-papers tools, and Ultra costs around $3,900 per year and adds onboarding and training, priority support, custom reporting, secret sessions, and advanced lead retrieval, with Sched claiming over $8,000 in bundled feature and savings value at that tier. An Enterprise tier is available with custom pricing for organizations that need on-premise hosting, custom feature development, or dedicated premium agent support. All plans include unlimited events, and smaller events (roughly under 50 attendees) can often run free or at reduced cost, with additional discounts offered for nonprofits (up to 50 percent off), educational institutions (33 percent), government organizations (17 percent), and startups (17 percent). Sched also offers a 30-day free trial and a 60-day money-back guarantee. Because pricing scales with plan tier and attendee volume rather than strictly per-seat, Sched tends to appeal most to conference and event organizers, associations, and marketing/events teams running recurring multi-track events rather than one-off small meetings. It is distinct from general-purpose scheduling and calendar-booking tools (such as appointment schedulers), focusing instead on the specialized workflow of building and publishing a full multi-day event agenda with speaker and sponsor management baked in.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Sched is event scheduling and agenda-management software founded in 2008 and headquartered in Los Angeles, California. It is designed for organizers of conferences, conventions, and festivals who need to publish multi-track schedules, manage speakers and sponsors, and let attendees build personalized agendas.
The platform serves both in-person and virtual/hybrid events, with a companion web and mobile app that attendees use to browse sessions, bookmark talks, and build their own schedule.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Sched provides personalized scheduling, speaker and session profile pages, registration and ticketing, call-for-papers/session submission tools, attendee networking, customizable badges, and sponsor/exhibitor profiles.
Higher tiers add attendance tracking, lead retrieval for sponsors, advanced call-for-papers workflows, custom reporting, and dedicated onboarding and training support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Sched is priced as annual plans rather than simple monthly subscriptions: Launch starts around $600 per year, Boost around $1,500 per year, and Ultra around $3,900 per year, with an Enterprise tier available at custom pricing for on-premise or highly customized deployments.
Discounts are available for nonprofits, educational institutions, government organizations, and startups, and Sched offers a 30-day free trial along with a 60-day money-back guarantee.

Free or low-cost options for smaller events under about 50 attendees

Enterprise features such as on-premise hosting require custom sales conversations
Less suited to simple one-on-one appointment scheduling than dedicated booking tools

faqs.question: What is Sched used for
faqs.answer: Sched is used to build and publish schedules for conferences, conventions, and festivals, letting attendees create personalized agendas and organizers manage speakers, sponsors, and registration.

faqs.question: How much does Sched cost
faqs.answer: Sched's Launch plan starts at roughly $600 per year, Boost is about $1,500 per year, and Ultra is about $3,900 per year, with custom Enterprise pricing available.

faqs.question: Does Sched offer a free trial
faqs.answer: Yes, Sched offers a 30-day free trial and a 60-day money-back guarantee on paid plans.

faqs.question: Is Sched suitable for small events
faqs.answer: Yes, Sched offers free or reduced pricing for smaller events, generally under about 50 attendees.

faqs.question: Does Sched support virtual and hybrid events
faqs.answer: Yes, Sched supports in-person, virtual, and hybrid event formats through its web and mobile app.

faqs.question: When was Sched founded
faqs.answer: Sched was founded in 2008 and is headquartered in Los Angeles, California.

faqs.question: Does Sched offer discounts
faqs.answer: Yes, Sched offers discounts for nonprofits (up to 50 percent), education (33 percent), government (17 percent), and startups (17 percent).

enrichment.short_description: Schemathesis is an open-source property-based API testing tool that automatically generates test cases from OpenAPI and GraphQL schemas to catch bugs and spec violations.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open source, with paid cloud add-ons
enrichment.starting_price: Free (open-source core); paid Workbench and TraceCov cloud offerings, pricing not publicly listed
enrichment.seo_title: Schemathesis Review 2026 - Open-Source API Testing Tool Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn how Schemathesis automatically generates API tests from OpenAPI and GraphQL schemas, its key features, and its open-source and cloud pricing options.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Schemathesis is an open-source, property-based API testing tool that automatically generates test cases directly from OpenAPI and GraphQL schemas rather than requiring engineers to hand-write test scenarios. It is built on top of Hypothesis, a Python property-based testing library, and uses it to generate large numbers of diverse, edge-case-covering inputs based on the constraints defined in an API's schema, then checks that the live API's responses conform to that schema and behave correctly under boundary and fuzzing conditions. The project's source code is hosted on GitHub at github.com/schemathesis/schemathesis, and it integrates into standard developer workflows through a pytest plugin, a command-line interface, a Docker image, and official GitHub Actions and GitLab CI integrations, making it straightforward to run automated schema-driven API tests as part of continuous integration pipelines. Schemathesis has an academic pedigree: research presented at ICSE 2022 found it detected meaningfully more defects (cited as 1.4x to 4.5x more) than comparable API testing tools in a controlled study. It has seen adoption at large engineering organizations including Spotify, WordPress, JetBrains, Red Hat, Netflix, SAP, IBM, and Capital One, the last of which has published its own engineering blog post describing how it uses Schemathesis to automate API testing. The core Schemathesis tool is free and open source with no licensing cost for basic API testing, which is its primary and most widely used offering. Beyond the open-source core, the project has begun offering commercial cloud services under the Schemathesis.io brand, including Workbench, an API fuzzing dashboard intended for continuous monitoring of API behavior over time, and TraceCov, a tool for schema-level test coverage analytics against an OpenAPI specification; specific pricing for these paid cloud services is not publicly published on the marketing site, and prospective customers need to contact the company or sign up to see plan details. For a directory like gappsy.com, Schemathesis fits best in categories such as API testing, developer tools, QA and test automation, and open-source software, and it is most relevant to backend engineers, QA engineers, and platform teams responsible for keeping REST and GraphQL APIs consistent with their published schemas.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Schemathesis is an open-source API testing tool that uses property-based testing to automatically generate test cases from OpenAPI and GraphQL schema definitions, rather than requiring engineers to write individual test scenarios by hand.

Built on the Hypothesis testing library, it is used by engineering teams at companies including Spotify, JetBrains, Red Hat, and Capital One to catch schema violations, edge-case bugs, and spec inconsistencies before they reach production.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The tool generates diverse, schema-aware test inputs automatically, validates that API responses conform to the OpenAPI or GraphQL specification, and performs schema-driven fuzzing to probe boundary values and constraint violations.
It integrates with pytest, the command line, Docker, and CI systems like GitHub Actions and GitLab CI, and produces reproducible failure reports including curl commands for easy debugging.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core Schemathesis tool is free and open source, available via pip, Docker, or a GitHub Action with no licensing cost for standard API testing use.

Schemathesis also offers paid cloud add-ons, Workbench (an API fuzzing dashboard for continuous monitoring) and TraceCov (schema-level coverage analytics), though specific pricing for these commercial services is not published and requires contacting the company directly.

features.title: Property-based test generation
features.description: Automatically generates diverse API test cases from OpenAPI and GraphQL schemas using the Hypothesis testing engine.

features.title: Schema conformance validation
features.description: Checks that live API responses match the constraints and types defined in the schema specification.

features.title: Schema-aware fuzzing
features.description: Probes boundary values and constraint violations derived directly from the API schema.

features.title: CI/CD integration
features.description: Official integrations with GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and pytest for automated testing pipelines.

features.title: Multiple deployment options
features.description: Available as a Python package, Docker image, or standalone CLI tool.

features.title: Reproducible failure reports
features.description: Generates curl commands and minimal reproduction cases to speed up debugging of failing tests.

features.title: Workbench cloud dashboard
features.description: A paid cloud offering for continuous API fuzzing monitoring beyond one-off CI runs.

features.title: TraceCov coverage analytics
features.description: A paid tool for measuring schema-level test coverage against an OpenAPI specification.

pros: Core tool is completely free and open source
Automatically generates large numbers of edge-case tests without manual scripting
Proven to catch more defects than some alternative tools in academic research
Strong CI/CD integration with GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and pytest
Adopted by major engineering organizations, indicating production reliability
cons: Requires an accurate, well-maintained OpenAPI or GraphQL schema to be effective
Pricing for the paid Workbench and TraceCov cloud offerings is not publicly listed
Primarily oriented toward Python-centric and CI-based workflows
Learning curve for teams unfamiliar with property-based testing concepts

faqs.question: Is Schemathesis free
faqs.answer: Yes, the core Schemathesis API testing tool is free and open source; paid cloud add-ons called Workbench and TraceCov are also available.

faqs.question: What does Schemathesis test
faqs.answer: Schemathesis tests REST APIs defined by OpenAPI schemas and GraphQL APIs by automatically generating test cases from the schema and checking for spec violations and bugs.

faqs.question: How does Schemathesis generate test cases
faqs.answer: It uses the Hypothesis property-based testing library to generate diverse, schema-constrained inputs, including boundary and edge cases, automatically.

faqs.question: Does Schemathesis integrate with CI/CD
faqs.answer: Yes, it has official integrations with GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and pytest for automated testing in continuous integration pipelines.

faqs.question: Who uses Schemathesis
faqs.answer: Companies including Spotify, JetBrains, Red Hat, Netflix, SAP, IBM, and Capital One have used or written about using Schemathesis.

faqs.question: What is Schemathesis Workbench
faqs.answer: Workbench is a paid cloud dashboard for continuous API fuzzing monitoring, offered alongside the free open-source core tool.

faqs.question: Can Schemathesis test GraphQL APIs
faqs.answer: Yes, Schemathesis supports generating tests from both OpenAPI and GraphQL schema definitions.

use_cases.title: Automated API regression testing
use_cases.description: Engineering teams run Schemathesis in CI to automatically catch schema violations and regressions before deployment.

use_cases.title: Pre-release API fuzz testing
use_cases.description: Teams use schema-driven fuzzing to find edge-case bugs and boundary violations before shipping an API to production.

use_cases.title: Continuous API monitoring
use_cases.description: Organizations use the paid Workbench cloud dashboard to continuously monitor live APIs for schema drift and failures.

features: Property-based test generation from OpenAPI/GraphQL schemas
GitHub Actions and GitLab CI integration
Schema-level test coverage analytics for OpenAPI specs

enrichment.short_description: Schleuder is a free, open-source GPG-encrypted mailing list manager that lets subscribers exchange OpenPGP-encrypted email and communicate securely with non-subscribers.
enrichment.seo_title: Schleuder Review 2026 - Open-Source Encrypted Mailing List Manager
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn how Schleuder provides GPG-encrypted mailing lists for privacy-focused groups, its features, self-hosting requirements, and open-source licensing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Schleuder is a free, open-source encrypted mailing list manager built specifically for groups that need to communicate over email with strong end-to-end privacy guarantees. Written in Ruby and released under the GNU General Public License version 3.0, it functions as a GPG-enabled mailing list gateway: it automatically decrypts incoming OpenPGP-encrypted messages, re-encrypts and distributes them to all subscribers on a list, and can also receive and re-encrypt messages sent by non-subscribers so that a group can safely correspond with outside contacts without exposing individual subscriber keys or requiring every participant to manually manage encryption. Version 3 of Schleuder represented a complete rewrite of the project aimed at making it more robust, flexible, and internationalized, and it introduced an API that can back an optional web interface (schleuder-web) for managing list settings and subscriptions. The project is developed and hosted primarily on the 0xacab.org GitLab instance run by the Riseup collective, with a mirror maintained on GitHub for visibility, and prebuilt packages are available for Debian (including current stable releases such as bullseye and bookworm), CentOS, and Arch Linux to simplify self-hosted installation. As with most privacy and activist-oriented infrastructure tools in this space, there is no commercial company behind Schleuder, no pricing tiers, and no vendor headquarters or employee count to report; it is maintained by volunteer contributors within the privacy and digital-security tooling community, historically associated with groups like Nadir and the broader European hacktivist and anti-surveillance infrastructure scene. Because it requires server administration skills to deploy (a mail transfer agent, Ruby runtime, and GPG key management), Schleuder is best suited to technically capable teams, activist organizations, journalists, NGOs, and privacy-conscious communities that need a secure group email channel and are willing to self-host rather than rely on a commercial encrypted communication SaaS product. For a directory like gappsy.com, Schleuder belongs in categories such as open-source software, email and communication privacy tools, and self-hosted infrastructure, and it should be described accurately as free software with no company behind it rather than assigned invented pricing or company facts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Schleuder is a free, open-source encrypted mailing list manager written in Ruby and licensed under GPL 3.0. It allows subscribers to exchange OpenPGP-encrypted email among themselves while also safely sending and receiving messages from non-subscribers through automatic decryption and re-encryption.

The project is developed on the 0xacab.org GitLab instance, with a mirror on GitHub, and is commonly used by activist groups, privacy-focused organizations, and technical teams that need a self-hosted, encrypted group email solution.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Schleuder automatically handles GPG encryption and decryption, header stripping, and format conversions for list traffic, removing the need for every subscriber to manage encryption manually for each message.
Version 3 introduced an internationalized, more robust rewrite along with an API that powers an optional web interface for managing list configuration and subscriptions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Schleuder is entirely free and open source, distributed under the GNU GPL 3.0 license with no commercial pricing tiers, subscriptions, or paid hosting offered by the project itself.

Because it is self-hosted software rather than a SaaS product, users are responsible for their own server, mail transfer agent, and ongoing maintenance costs, but there is no license fee to use Schleuder.

features.title: Automatic GPG encryption and decryption
features.description: Handles OpenPGP encryption and decryption of list traffic automatically so subscribers do not need to manage keys per message.

features.title: Remailing to non-subscribers
features.description: Can receive and forward encrypted messages to and from people who are not list subscribers, re-encrypting as needed.

features.title: Header stripping and format conversion
features.description: Strips potentially identifying headers and normalizes message formats to reduce metadata leakage.

features.title: Optional web interface
features.description: Version 3's API can power schleuder-web, an optional browser-based interface for list administration.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Runs on your own infrastructure, with packages available for Debian, CentOS, and Arch Linux.

features.title: Internationalized, robust core
features.description: The version 3 rewrite focused on robustness and internationalization for broader adoption.

pros: Completely free and open source with no licensing cost
Strong end-to-end encryption model for sensitive group communication
Can safely bridge encrypted communication with non-subscribers
Available as prebuilt packages for major Linux distributions
Backed by an established privacy-tooling community (0xacab.org / Riseup infrastructure)
cons: Requires self-hosting and server administration skills to deploy
No commercial support, SLA, or vendor to contact for help
No official cloud-hosted version offered by the project
Smaller user base and documentation compared to mainstream mailing list tools
Ongoing GPG key management still required at the list-admin level

faqs.question: Is Schleuder free to use
faqs.answer: Yes, Schleuder is free, open-source software released under the GNU GPL 3.0 license with no licensing cost.

faqs.question: What does Schleuder do
faqs.answer: Schleuder is a GPG-enabled mailing list manager that automatically encrypts and decrypts messages so subscribers can exchange OpenPGP-encrypted email securely.

faqs.question: Do I need to self-host Schleuder
faqs.answer: Yes, Schleuder is self-hosted software; there is no official managed cloud version offered by the project.

faqs.question: What programming language is Schleuder written in
faqs.answer: Schleuder is written in Ruby.

faqs.question: Can Schleuder communicate with people who are not on the list
faqs.answer: Yes, Schleuder can receive and send messages to non-subscribers, re-encrypting traffic as needed to keep subscriber communication secure.

faqs.question: Where is Schleuder developed
faqs.answer: Schleuder is developed primarily on the 0xacab.org GitLab instance, with a mirror available on GitHub.

faqs.question: Does Schleuder have a web interface
faqs.answer: An optional web interface called schleuder-web can be used alongside Schleuder's API, introduced with the version 3 rewrite.

enrichment.short_description: Scoold is a self-hosted, open-source Q&A and knowledge-sharing platform for teams, modeled after Stack Overflow, with a free community edition and paid Pro tiers.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free open-source core with one-time license fees for Pro tiers and subscription-based cloud hosting
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Community edition); Scoold Pro from EUR 499 one-time per instance
enrichment.seo_title: Scoold Review 2026 - Self-Hosted Q&A Platform Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how Scoold's Stack Overflow-style Q&A platform works, its free open-source edition, Pro license pricing, and Scoold Cloud hosting options.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Scoold is a self-hosted, open-source Q&A and knowledge-sharing platform designed to give internal teams a Stack Overflow-style experience for their own questions, answers, and documentation. It is developed by Erudika and traces back to a project originally created in 2008 as a social network for schools, which was released in 2012, then refactored, repackaged, and fully open-sourced in 2017 under the Apache 2.0 license. The platform is deployable anywhere, including Heroku, DigitalOcean, AWS, Azure, or any VPS, and ships as a Docker container, a runnable JAR, or a native executable, giving teams flexibility over where they host their knowledge base. Core functionality includes full-text search, social login and OAuth 2.0 or LDAP authentication, custom badges and gamification, theming and white-label branding, backup and restore tools, email notifications, unlimited users, and a REST API for integrations, all included free in the open-source Community edition. Scoold's commercial model is built around one-time license fees rather than recurring subscriptions for its self-hosted Pro tiers: Scoold Pro costs EUR 499 as a one-time fee per instance and adds SAML 2.0 single sign-on, SCIM user provisioning, @mentions, file uploads, integrations with Microsoft Teams, Mattermost, and Slack, advanced security notifications, unlimited spaces and admins, approved domain restrictions, and account suspension controls, with free updates and support for 12 months after purchase. A higher Pro + Source tier costs EUR 1,999 as a one-time fee and additionally grants full source code access via Git, allows custom feature development, and includes architecture consulting along with VAT invoicing for businesses. For teams that don't want to self-host, Scoold Cloud offers managed hosting of Scoold Pro on a subscription basis, though exact cloud subscription pricing is not published on the main pricing page and requires contacting the vendor. All Pro licenses come with a 14-day money-back guarantee, and every tier supports unlimited users regardless of price. This one-time-license-plus-optional-cloud model differentiates Scoold from most modern SaaS knowledge-base tools, making it particularly attractive to engineering and IT teams that prefer to self-host internal tools, avoid per-seat SaaS costs at scale, and want full control and eventual source-code ownership over their internal Q&A platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Scoold is a self-hosted, open-source Q&A and knowledge-sharing platform built by Erudika, modeled on the Stack Overflow experience but designed for internal team use. The underlying project dates back to 2008 and 2012, and was fully open-sourced under Apache 2.0 in 2017.
It can be deployed on Heroku, DigitalOcean, AWS, Azure, or any VPS as a Docker container, JAR, or native executable, giving engineering and IT teams full control over hosting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The free Community edition includes full-text search, social login/OAuth2/LDAP, custom badges, theming, backup and restore, email notifications, unlimited users, and a REST API.
Paid Pro tiers add SAML 2.0 SSO, SCIM provisioning, @mentions, file uploads, integrations with Teams, Mattermost, and Slack, advanced security notifications, and unlimited spaces and admins.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Scoold uses one-time license pricing rather than per-seat subscriptions for self-hosted tiers: Scoold Pro costs EUR 499 one-time per instance, and Pro + Source costs EUR 1,999 one-time per instance and includes full source code access.

Scoold Cloud offers managed, subscription-based hosting of Scoold Pro for teams that prefer not to self-host, though specific cloud subscription prices are not listed publicly and require contacting the vendor.

features.description: Social login, OAuth 2.0, and LDAP support for integrating with existing identity systems.

features.description: Enterprise single sign-on via SAML 2.0 and automated user provisioning via SCIM on Pro tiers.

features.title: Chat and collaboration integrations
features.description: Integrations with Microsoft Teams, Mattermost, and Slack available on Pro tiers.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment flexibility
features.description: Deployable via Docker, JAR, or native executable on Heroku, AWS, Azure, DigitalOcean, or any VPS.

features.description: A full REST API for integrating Scoold data with other internal tools.

features.title: Source code access (Pro + Source)
features.description: The top license tier grants full Git access to Scoold's source code for custom development.

pros: Free, fully-featured open-source Community edition with no per-user fees
One-time license pricing avoids recurring per-seat SaaS costs at scale
Flexible self-hosting on any cloud provider or VPS
Enterprise features like SAML SSO and SCIM available on Pro
Pro + Source gives full source code ownership for custom development
cons: Requires self-hosting and server maintenance unless using Scoold Cloud
Scoold Cloud subscription pricing is not publicly listed
Support for Pro licenses is limited to 12 months of free updates post-purchase
Smaller ecosystem and community compared to mainstream SaaS Q&A/helpdesk tools

faqs.answer: Yes, the Scoold Community edition is free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license; paid Pro tiers add enterprise features.

faqs.question: How much does Scoold Pro cost
faqs.answer: Scoold Pro costs EUR 499 as a one-time fee per instance, and Pro + Source costs EUR 1,999 one-time, both with 12 months of free updates and support.

faqs.question: Does Scoold offer cloud hosting
faqs.answer: Yes, Scoold Cloud offers managed subscription-based hosting of Scoold Pro, though specific pricing requires contacting the vendor.

faqs.question: Who develops Scoold
faqs.answer: Scoold is developed by Erudika; the project was open-sourced under Apache 2.0 in 2017.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Scoold
faqs.answer: Yes, Scoold can be self-hosted via Docker, JAR, or native executable on Heroku, AWS, Azure, DigitalOcean, or any VPS.

faqs.question: What is Pro + Source
faqs.answer: Pro + Source is Scoold's top license tier, costing EUR 1,999 one-time, which includes full source code access via Git along with all Pro features.

faqs.question: Does Scoold support single sign-on
faqs.answer: Yes, SAML 2.0 SSO and SCIM provisioning are available on Scoold Pro and Pro + Source license tiers.

enrichment.short_description: ScoreApp is a quiz funnel marketing platform that helps businesses build interactive quizzes and scorecards to generate leads, qualify prospects, and drive sales.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium with tiered monthly/annual subscription plans
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan available; Starter plan from $39/month (or $30/month billed annually)
enrichment.seo_title: ScoreApp Review 2026 - Quiz Funnel Marketing Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how ScoreApp's quiz funnel marketing platform works, its pricing plans from free to Enterprise, and its AI-powered quiz and lead generation features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ScoreApp is a quiz funnel marketing platform built by Hyper Targeted Marketing Limited, a company incorporated in November 2019 and based in Watford, England, and co-founded by Steven Oddy and Daniel Priestley. The product is designed to help businesses attract, qualify, and convert leads through interactive quizzes and scorecards rather than static lead-capture forms, supporting the full customer journey from initial attraction through onboarding and ongoing engagement. Core capabilities include an AI quiz builder and AI marketing assistant, unlimited quiz questions, jump logic for branching question paths, multiple question types, unlimited landing pages, dynamic personalized result pages, and CRM connectivity for pushing captured leads into a business's existing sales and marketing stack. ScoreApp uses a freemium pricing model with five tiers. The Free plan allows one live scorecard and up to 10 monthly responses. The Starter plan costs $39 per month billed monthly or about $30 per month billed annually (a 25 percent annual discount), supporting 3 live scorecards, 100 monthly responses, and 1 user, and adds unlimited quiz questions, jump logic, and export capabilities. The Business plan, marketed as the best-value tier, costs $99 per month monthly or about $75 per month annually, supporting 10 live scorecards, 1,000 monthly responses, and 3 users, and adds a custom domain, abandonment emails, advanced insights, custom CSS and scripts, Google Analytics and Facebook Pixel integration, dynamic PDF reports, and audience segmentation. The Pro plan costs $149 per month monthly or about $112 per month annually, supporting 30 live scorecards, 3,000 monthly responses, and 5 users, and adds data enrichment and split testing on top of everything in Business. An Enterprise plan starts around $799 per month with custom, infinitely scalable pricing, unlimited user seats, VIP support, tailored onboarding, granular permissions, and single sign-on. All paid plans come with a 14-day money-back guarantee. ScoreApp has reportedly grown to roughly $3.2 million in annual recurring revenue without raising outside venture capital funding, according to third-party revenue-tracking sources. It is most relevant to marketers, coaches, consultants, and service businesses looking to use interactive quiz-based lead generation and qualification funnels rather than traditional static forms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ScoreApp is a quiz funnel marketing platform built by Hyper Targeted Marketing Limited, founded in 2019 by Steven Oddy and Daniel Priestley and based in Watford, England. It helps businesses build interactive quizzes and scorecards to attract, qualify, and convert leads.

The platform is aimed at marketers, coaches, consultants, and service businesses that want to replace static lead-capture forms with engaging, data-driven quiz experiences tied into their existing CRM and marketing stack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: ScoreApp includes an AI quiz builder and AI marketing assistant, unlimited quiz questions, jump logic for branching paths, multiple question types, unlimited landing pages, dynamic personalized result pages, and CRM connectivity across all plans, including the free tier.

Higher tiers add custom domains, abandonment emails, advanced insights and audience segmentation, custom CSS/scripts, analytics pixel integrations, dynamic PDF reports, data enrichment, and split testing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ScoreApp offers a free plan (1 live scorecard, 10 monthly responses) plus four paid tiers: Starter at $39/month, Business at $99/month, Pro at $149/month, and a custom-priced Enterprise plan starting around $799/month, each with roughly a 25 percent discount for annual billing.
All paid plans include a 14-day money-back guarantee, and plan limits scale by number of live scorecards, monthly responses, and included user seats.

enrichment.short_description: Scoro is an all-in-one work and professional services automation platform combining project management, billing, and reporting for agencies and consultancies.
enrichment.pricing_model: Per-user monthly subscription with tiered plans, minimum seat requirement
enrichment.seo_title: Scoro Review 2026 - Work Management Software Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Compare Scoro's project management, billing, and resource planning plans, pricing per user, and key features for agencies and consultancies.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Scoro is an all-in-one work and professional services automation (PSA) platform aimed at consultancies, agencies, architecture firms, and IT companies that need to manage projects, resources, and finances within a single integrated system rather than stitching together separate project management, time tracking, and invoicing tools. The company was founded in 2013 in Tallinn, Estonia, by Fred Krieger, and is now headquartered in London, England, with additional offices in New York, Tallinn, Riga, and Vilnius. Scoro has been recognized as one of the fastest-growing technology companies in Central Europe by the Deloitte Technology Fast 500 and has appeared on Inc. magazine's Inc. 5000 list of fastest-growing private US companies; the company raised a $16.4 million Series B funding round in 2021 led by Kennet Partners, reflecting continued investor confidence in its growth. Scoro's product spans project and task management, Gantt charts, quoting, invoicing, contact and CRM management, dashboards, and work reporting at its base tier, then layers on more advanced professional-services functionality as customers move up its plan structure, including project budgets, retainer management, role-based labor costing, financial reporting, resource planning, timesheets, price lists, revenue forecasting, sales pipeline management, and supplier management. Pricing is structured as four tiers billed per user per month, with a minimum of 5 user seats required on every plan: Core starts at $19.90 per user per month billed monthly (or $23.90 with certain billing terms), Growth is $32.90 per user per month, Performance is $49.90 per user per month, and Enterprise is available at custom pricing with added features like company-wide budgets, approval flows, multi-account reporting, single sign-on, user provisioning, and FTP integration. Scoro also sells optional premium onboarding services, which start at $1,699 for a six-week structured onboarding engagement, and it offers a 14-day free trial with no credit card required, along with support for multiple billing currencies including USD, EUR, GBP, AUD, and CAD. Because pricing is strictly per-seat with a 5-user minimum, Scoro is generally positioned for small-to-midsize teams and agencies rather than solo freelancers, competing most directly with other PSA and agency management platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Scoro is a professional services automation (PSA) and work management platform founded in 2013 in Tallinn, Estonia by Fred Krieger, now headquartered in London, England with offices in New York, Tallinn, Riga, and Vilnius. It targets consultancies, agencies, and IT firms that need projects, resourcing, and finances managed in one system.
The company raised a $16.4 million Series B round in 2021 led by Kennet Partners and has been recognized on both the Deloitte Technology Fast 500 and the Inc. 5000 lists of fast-growing companies.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Scoro combines project and task management, Gantt charts, quoting and invoicing, CRM/contact management, dashboards, and work reporting as its baseline capability set.
Higher tiers add project budgeting, retainer management, resource planning, timesheets, revenue forecasting, sales pipeline management, and supplier management for more advanced agency operations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Scoro prices per user per month across four tiers: Core at $19.90, Growth at $32.90, and Performance at $49.90, each requiring a minimum of 5 user seats, with a custom-priced Enterprise tier for larger organizations.
Optional premium onboarding starts at $1,699 for a six-week engagement, and Scoro offers a 14-day free trial with no credit card required.

enrichment.short_description: Scout APM, now branded Scout Monitoring, is an application performance monitoring tool that tracks errors, logs, and traces for Ruby, Python, PHP, Node.js, and Elixir applications.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium with usage-based (transaction volume) paid tiers
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan available (300K monthly transactions); paid plans from $19/month
enrichment.seo_title: Scout APM Review 2026 - Application Performance Monitoring Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Explore Scout APM (Scout Monitoring) pricing tiers, transaction-based plans, and features for Ruby, Python, PHP, Node.js, and Elixir performance monitoring.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Scout APM, now marketed as Scout Monitoring, is an application performance monitoring (APM) tool founded in 2008 and headquartered in Denver, Colorado, with a small team of roughly 11 to 50 employees as a privately held company. It focuses on helping developer teams find and fix performance problems in production applications, historically specializing in Ruby on Rails monitoring and later expanding to cover Python, PHP, Node.js, and Elixir. Since its founding, Scout has been used by SaaS companies such as Expedia, Chargify, Liftopia, and MindBody to track and isolate issues like N+1 database queries, memory bloat, and other performance abnormalities that are common but hard to diagnose in web application backends. In June 2021, the product expanded beyond pure performance tracing to add an integrated error monitoring solution, combining APM and error insights into a single dashboard, which aligns with its current Scout Monitoring branding covering errors, logs, and traces together. Pricing is transaction-volume based rather than per-seat, meaning all plans include unlimited users and unlimited applications, with cost scaling by how many monitored transactions, error events, and log storage a team needs. The Free plan includes 300,000 monthly transactions, 1 GB of log storage, and 5,000 errors at no cost. The flagship 'Production Ready' plan costs $59 per month billed annually (or $98 per month billed monthly) and includes 1.2 million monthly transactions, 50,000 errors, 30 GB of log storage with 14-day retention, and priority support. Scout also offers a build-your-own plan structure with named tiers -- Startup at $19/month (600K transactions), Small at $39/month (1.2M transactions), Medium at $99/month (3M transactions), and Large at $299/month (9M transactions) -- each with 5,000 included errors and 1 GB of logs, plus add-ons for additional errors ($19/month for 50,000 more) and additional log storage ($40/month for 30 GB more). An Enterprise tier offers custom transaction volume, custom log retention, SSO, security audits, and SLA reporting at custom pricing. Billing is month-to-month with no long-term contracts and the ability to cancel anytime, and annual billing saves roughly 10 percent versus monthly. This flexible, transaction-based, unlimited-seat pricing model differentiates Scout from per-host or per-seat APM competitors like Datadog or New Relic, making it particularly appealing to smaller engineering teams that want predictable APM costs without per-developer licensing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Scout APM, now branded Scout Monitoring, is an application performance monitoring tool founded in 2008 and headquartered in Denver, Colorado. It began with a strong focus on Ruby on Rails monitoring and has since expanded to support Python, PHP, Node.js, and Elixir applications.

The company is privately held with an estimated 11 to 50 employees, and its customers have historically included SaaS companies such as Expedia, Chargify, Liftopia, and MindBody. In June 2021, Scout added integrated error monitoring alongside its core performance tracing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Scout Monitoring tracks application traces, errors, and logs in a single dashboard, with particular strength in isolating N+1 database queries and memory bloat issues common in Rails and other web frameworks.

All plans, including the free tier, include unlimited users and unlimited monitored applications, with cost scaling purely by transaction volume, error count, and log storage rather than per-seat licensing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Scout offers a free plan with 300,000 monthly transactions, 5,000 errors, and 1 GB of logs, and a 'Production Ready' plan at $59/month (billed annually) or $98/month (billed monthly) with 1.2 million transactions and 30 GB of log storage.

Additional build-your-own tiers range from Startup at $19/month up to Large at $299/month based on transaction volume, with add-ons available for extra errors and log storage, plus a custom-priced Enterprise tier with SSO and SLA reporting.

features.title: Application performance traces
features.description: Tracks response times and performance bottlenecks across Ruby, Python, PHP, Node.js, and Elixir applications.

features.title: N+1 query and memory bloat detection
features.description: Specialized detection for common performance issues like N+1 database queries and memory bloat.

features.title: Integrated error monitoring
features.description: Combines error tracking with performance traces in a single dashboard since its June 2021 expansion.

features.title: Log management
features.description: Centralized log storage and search alongside performance and error data.

features.title: Unlimited users and applications
features.description: All plans, including free, support unlimited team members and monitored applications.

features.title: Transaction-based pricing tiers
features.description: Costs scale by monthly transaction volume rather than per-seat or per-host fees.

features.title: Priority and premier support
features.description: Priority support on the Production Ready plan and premier support with SLA reporting on Enterprise.

features.title: SSO and security audits (Enterprise)
features.description: Enterprise plan adds single sign-on, custom retention, and formal security audits.

pros: Unlimited users and applications on every plan, including free
Transaction-based pricing avoids per-seat cost scaling
Specialized, mature support for Ruby on Rails plus other major languages
Combines APM, error monitoring, and logs in a single product
Free tier available with a meaningful transaction allowance
cons: Log retention on paid non-enterprise plans is limited to 14 days
Errors are capped at 5,000 on most tiers unless purchasing add-ons
Smaller feature breadth than large APM platforms like Datadog or New Relic
Enterprise-grade features such as SSO and SLA reporting require custom Enterprise pricing

faqs.question: What is Scout APM used for
faqs.answer: Scout APM (Scout Monitoring) is used to monitor application performance, errors, and logs for Ruby, Python, PHP, Node.js, and Elixir applications.

faqs.question: Is Scout APM free
faqs.answer: Yes, Scout offers a free plan with 300,000 monthly transactions, 5,000 errors, and 1 GB of log storage.

faqs.question: How is Scout APM priced
faqs.answer: Scout is priced based on monthly transaction volume rather than per user, with plans ranging from $19/month to $299/month plus a custom Enterprise tier.

faqs.question: When was Scout APM founded
faqs.answer: Scout APM was founded in 2008 and is headquartered in Denver, Colorado.

faqs.question: Does Scout APM support Rails specifically
faqs.answer: Yes, Scout has specialized in Ruby on Rails performance monitoring since its founding, including detecting N+1 queries and memory bloat.

faqs.question: Does Scout APM include error monitoring
faqs.answer: Yes, Scout added integrated error monitoring alongside performance tracing in June 2021.

faqs.question: Is there a limit on users with Scout APM plans
faqs.answer: No, all Scout plans, including the free tier, include unlimited users and unlimited applications.
